planting.note: Plant divisions or container-grown specimens in moist, slightly acidic to neutral, humus-rich, well-drained soil in partial to full shade in early fall; drumstick primrose requires consistently moist soil during its spring growing season and appreciates light mulch — it goes semi-dormant in summer heat and benefits from drier conditions then.

directions: Plant divisions in moist, slightly acidic to neutral, humus-rich soil in partial to full shade; drumstick primrose is an unusual and distinctive Himalayan spring primrose that differs from all other primroses in its flower arrangement — rather than flat clusters or single flowers, it produces perfectly spherical, drumstick-like, ball-shaped flower heads on upright stalks, densely packed with small, purple, lilac, pink, rose-red, or white flowers that bloom in early to mid-spring, often pushing through the last snow of the season. The bold, spherical flower heads create a strong visual statement quite different from the low, spreading flower clusters of common primroses, and the contrast of the round, upright flowers with the rosette of large, coarsely toothed, wrinkled leaves creates an architectural quality. One of the most cold-hardy of all ornamental primroses (to zone 2) and effective in large drifts under deciduous trees where snow melt provides the moisture conditions it needs. 'Alba' (white), 'Grandiflora' (large purple heads), and 'Ruby' (deep red) are standard cultivars.
sources: North Carolina State Extension — Plants Database

planting.note: Plant divisions or container-grown specimens in moist, strongly acidic to slightly acidic (pH 5.0–6.5), humus-rich, well-drained soil in partial to full shade in spring; kirengeshoma is intolerant of alkaline or dry soil and resents drought — consistent moisture and acidic conditions are non-negotiable for successful cultivation.

directions: Plant divisions in moist, strongly acidic, humus-rich soil in partial to full shade; yellow waxbells is a Korean and Japanese shade perennial of exceptional refinement — an elegant plant with large, soft, palmate, maple-like leaves on dark-purple stems and, uniquely among shade perennials, producing nodding clusters of distinctive, thick-petaled, waxy, pale-yellow bell-shaped flowers in late summer and early autumn (August–September), at the tail end of the shade garden season when most shade perennials have completed their flowering. The flowers have a subtly understated quality — pale, pure yellow, waxy, and perfect — that perfectly complements the architectural plant habit. It requires the cool, moist, acidic conditions of a Japanese woodland garden to perform at its best; it resents alkaline soil and dryness and will not perform satisfactorily in the South. The related Korean species Kirengeshoma koreana (synonym K. palmata 'Koreana') is slightly more upright and open in habit. An exceptional plant for the connoisseur shade garden in zones 5–8 with naturally acidic, cool, moist conditions.
sources: North Carolina State Extension — Plants Database

planting.note: Plant divisions or container-grown specimens in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, average to humus-rich soil in partial to full shade in spring; mukdenia is drought-tolerant once established for a shade plant and performs better in drier conditions than many moisture-loving shade perennials.

directions: Plant divisions in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, humus-rich soil in partial to full shade; mukdenia is a Korean and northeastern Chinese shade perennial of the family Saxifragaceae — related to bergenia and heuchera — that produces bold, deeply lobed, glossy-green, maple-like leaves arranged in spreading, basal rosettes that provide an attractive, textural groundcover in the shaded garden. In spring, small clusters of white, star-shaped flowers appear on upright stems before the leaves fully expand. The primary ornamental season extends beyond spring, however, because in autumn the leaves develop brilliant and extensive red, orange, and bronze coloration from the leaf margins inward, creating one of the finest autumn foliage effects of any shade garden perennial — the red-edged, green-centered leaves are striking and long-lasting. More drought-tolerant than most shade perennials. 'Crimson Fans' is the most widely grown selection, with the most vivid and extensive autumn reddening. Excellent as an edging plant in shaded borders.
sources: North Carolina State Extension — Plants Database

planting.note: Plant divisions in moist, slightly acidic to neutral, humus-rich soil in partial to full shade in early fall or very early spring; large-leaved pennycress spreads steadily by self-seeding and rhizomes to form a dense weed-suppressing groundcover — a single planting typically produces a self-maintaining colony within 3–4 years.

directions: Plant divisions in moist, slightly acidic to neutral, humus-rich soil in partial to full shade; large-leaved pennycress is a Caucasian evergreen shade groundcover belonging to the mustard family that is one of the earliest and most reliable shade garden performers of the year — its clusters of small, white, four-petaled, cross-shaped flowers open in very early spring (February-March) before most perennials have emerged, covering the plant with a fresh white display at the same time as snowdrops and early crocuses. The large, thick, kidney-shaped, glossy, dark-green leaves remain attractive throughout the year and create a dense, low-maintenance evergreen groundcover in deeply shaded conditions. It spreads by both self-seeding and creeping rhizomes to form gradually expanding, weed-suppressing colonies under trees, where it requires virtually no maintenance beyond initial establishment. An outstanding and underused shade groundcover for zones 5–9, it tolerates dry shade better than many competing groundcovers once established.
sources: North Carolina State Extension — Plants Database

planting.note: Plant divisions or container-grown specimens in deep, well-drained, slightly alkaline to neutral, fertile soil in full sun to partial shade in spring; giant sea kale develops a deep, fleshy taproot and deeply resents transplanting once established — site carefully, as the taproot can penetrate 3–4 feet into the soil.

directions: Plant divisions in deep, well-drained, slightly alkaline to neutral, fertile soil in full sun to partial shade; giant sea kale (ornamental crambe, Caucasian sea kale) is one of the most impressively bold and architecturally dramatic perennials available for large-scale garden use — in spring it produces an enormous basal mound of massive, dark-blue-green, deeply lobed, cabbage-like leaves, then erupts in early summer into a huge, branching, cloud-like flower structure rising 4–6 feet and spreading nearly as wide, composed of millions of tiny, honey-fragrant, pure-white flowers arranged in a vast, airy, billowing dome that creates a gypsophila-on-a-giant-scale effect. The effect in full flower is one of the most impressive of any garden plant — a living fountain of white filling a space the size of a small car. After flowering the seed pods create an additional season of interest. Excellent as an extraordinary back-of-border specimen or as a focal point in large herbaceous gardens; requires considerable space. The fleshy taproot penetrates deeply and the plant resents disturbance once established.
sources: North Carolina State Extension — Plants Database
